title: ‘Camp’ Review: Guilt Is the Villain in a Transfixing, Grief-Stricken Tale of Sisterhood and Witchcraft
description: Emily (Zola Grimmer) can’t help but be forthcoming when, in a juvenile game of truth or dare at a party, she’s asked to confess her biggest regret.
body:
Emily (Zola Grimmer) can’t help but be forthcoming when, in a juvenile game of truth or dare at a party, she’s asked to confess her biggest regret. She first opts for a harmless revelation, but when probed, she admits to having taken someone’s life by accident. The night will end with another unexpected death under her watch. That’s the tragic setup to Avalon Fast’s “Camp,” a transfixing and tonally mischievous tale of sisterhood forged from trauma, grief and witchcraft. But before the explicitly otherworldly takes over, a despondent Emily arrives at a summer camp for troubled children where she’ll work as one of the counselors. It’s ironic that she’ll be providing support to younger kids in her state. The hope is that being among peers in darkness might bring out some light.
	

	All the girls Emily meets at the camp have been attending for years, but they quickly embrace her, especially the vivacious Rosie (Cherry Moore) and Clara (Alice Wordsworth), the group’s alluring de facto leader. From there, “Camp” evokes “The Craft” with a dose of the esoteric, forest-fairy aesthetic that the musical act Florence + the Machine has for long procured as their signature — but in an indie film context that allows for evocative formal inventiveness. Fast cultivates eeriness, but also a sense of enigmatic wonder that, while decidedly unsubtle, is welcome: Shining stars that at times take over the frame and  fantastically artificial backdrops place the narrative in an interstitial space between reality and an intoxicating, dream-like realm.


	Grimmer’s expertly calibrated turn as Emily presents the character as eager for connection and at first comfortable enough to surrender to drunken nights and camaraderie without the need to divulge her secrets. Her unraveling, from sadness to despair to acceptance and ultimately empowerment, feels earned, as the pendulum of emotions swings from side to side on more than one occasion until she reaches a catharsis.
	

	The kids at camp are little more than an afterthought, except for one, Eden (Izza Jarvis), whose instability triggers Emily. Otherwise, the girls’ lack of concern for what should be their priority is compelling: They’re here to work on themselves through, as we later discover, self-determining sorcery but also to indulge in the carelessness of youth.

	Fast makes no concessions to the male characters, committing fully to an appreciation of supportive girlhood, at the expense of the other gender. Emily questions such disregard for men, but eventually accepts it as part of the pact to prioritize herself and her newfound sorority. It’s a fitting transaction that feeds into the playful malevolence of their practice. Still, Emily’s strongest bond is with her father, whom she calls repeatedly and to whom she speaks honestly about her struggles, even if he can’t fully understand her pain.

	Dan (Austyn Van de Kamp), the God-fearing leader of the operation, loses his virginity to Nev (Lea Rose Sebastianis), another of the girls, during a drunken night. His deflowering serves as sacrifice for the feminine force that grants these young women powers. Later, another nerdy boy will fall prey to their antics, yet there’s no gruesome violence on sight, only a melancholic and ethereal exorcizing of the demons that plague Emily’s heart. Few details are ever shared about what the other girls have endured. It’s as if the longer they spend at camp, the more distant those sorrows become.

	One could indeed refer to what Emily and her friends are doing as “practical magic,” since the consequence of their dabbling with the occult is the rebuilding of their self-esteem and a reframing of how they understand the experiences that have shaped them. Thankfully, however, Fast doesn’t reach this in a straightforward, conventional manner. Instead, the prolific, up-and-coming filmmaker luxuriates in dances under the moonlight, fiery rituals and glimpses of memory, conceived with an ethereal quality in collaboration with cinematographer Eily Sprungman.

	Though in a far more delicate register, “Camp” does exist in a similar space as Jane Schoenbrun’s “Teenage Sex and Death at Camp Miasma” — not only because of their shared setting, but because they cleverly usurp the modes and tropes of horror and the supernatural to dissect humanistic concerns rather than solely exploiting them for gory diversion. And they do so with a gendered consciousness that’s imperative for the impact of their thematic focus.  
	

	With “Camp,” there’s a clarity of emotion if not of the mechanisms behind the witchcraft. It’s the mood that matters, an atmosphere of unpredictability and anticipation, but not exactly of danger — like listening to a slightly terrifying tale by the campfire with an open sky above in the company of people you are certain care about you. Once Emily commits to stepping away from what tethered her to her past, a new freedom, and maybe new fears,  emerge. She’s no longer swallowed by tragedy. A new loving force holds her up.

schema.headline: ‘Camp’ Review: Guilt Is the Villain in a Transfixing, Grief-Stricken Tale of Sisterhood and Witchcraft

title: ‘Masters of the Universe’ Sets Prime Video Streaming Release Date
description: “Masters of the Universe” will be available to stream on Prime Video starting July 22. The movie had a disappointing run at the summer box office wi
body:
“Masters of the Universe” will be available to stream on Prime Video starting July 22. The movie had a disappointing run at the summer box office with just $113 million worldwide. 

	The film is based on the universe created by Mattel in 1982 and marks the franchise’s second live-action adaption, with the first premiering in 1987 and starring Dolph Lundgren.

	Nicholas Galitzine plays Adam Glenn, aka He-Man, who must protect his home planet of Eternia from the evil forces of Skeletor, played by Jared Leto. The film also stars Camila Mendes as Teela, Idris Elba as Man-at-Arms, Alison Brie, James Purefoy, Charlotte Riley, Morena Baccarin, Artie Wilkinson-Hunt, Eire Farrell and more.
	

	Travis Knight directed the movie with Todd Black, Jason Blumenthal, Robbie Brenner and DeVon Franklin producing. Chris Butler penned the script, who joined after initial drafts were written by David Callaham and Aaron and Adam Nee.

	“Masters of the Universe” premiered in theaters on June 5 and earned $29.3 million in North America for its opening weekend. It debuted at No. 2, behind “Scary Movie,” and cost about $200 million to produce. The film was initially tossed around multiple studios, including Netflix, Warner Bros. and Sony Pictures, before Amazon MGM purchased its rights in 2024.

	This is far from Galitizine’s first collaboration with Amazon MGM Studio, having previously starred in the adaptation of “Red, White & Royal Blue” and alongside Anne Hathaway in “The Idea of You.” The actor was also featured in Netflix’s “Purple Hearts” and Amazon’s “Cinderella,” which was led by Camila Cabello. Galitizine will reprise his role as England’s Prince Harry for the “Red, White & Royal Blue” sequel, “Red, White & Royal Wedding.”

schema.headline: ‘Masters of the Universe’ Sets Prime Video Streaming Release Date

title: Brunello Cucinelli Docufilm ‘The Gracious Visionary’ Sells Internationally Ahead of North American Release (EXCLUSIVE)
description: Oscar-winning director Giuseppe Tornatore‘s docufilm “Brunello: The Gracious Visionary” — about Brunello Cucinelli, the fashion designer known as It
body:
Oscar-winning director Giuseppe Tornatore‘s docufilm “Brunello: The Gracious Visionary” — about Brunello Cucinelli, the fashion designer known as Italy’s “King of Cashmere” — has been sold to a slew of territories, expanding the film’s global footprint. 

	Sales company Twenty-Nine Palms Entertainment has clinched deals that will now see the docufilm released to audiences across Europe, Asia, and the Middle East, following its successful theatrical outing in Italy last year and ahead of upcoming releases in the U.K., Ireland and North America on July 24th.
	

	“Brunello: The Gracious Visionary” also recently screened at the Shanghai Film Festival with Brunello Cucinelli in tow.


	“Brunello: The Gracious Visionary” chronicles Cucinelli’s rise from being a farmer’s son to becoming the creative director and executive chairman of a global luxury lifestyle brand with a market capitalization of more than $7.8 billion. The elegant film stars “Love & Gelato” breakout Saul Nanni as a younger version of Cucinelli and features a score by Oscar-winning composer Nicola Piovani (“Life Is Beautiful”).

	“The Gracious Visionary” interweaves documentary and reenacted components to tell the story of how his mindset was shaped by learning philosophy, playing cards and the generosity of others.

	Cucinelli, who was born in the ancient Umbrian hamlet of Castel Rigone in 1953, founded his company in 1978 and became successful by dyeing cashmere in “pop colors” at a time when, as he has put it, there was no colored cashmere for women. The docufilm, which was shot in Umbria, depicts key moments in his career, showcasing his passion for Italian craftsmanship and commitment to a concept he calls “humanistic capitalism,” under which every stitch of clothing his company creates is made in Italy and mostly in the area where he comes from. Cucinelli also makes sure his factory workers are paid fair wages and feel like family. He has completely renovated the Italian village of Solomeo that is home to his headquarters.
	

	Twenty-Nine Palms Entertainment has now sold the title to: Autumn Sun Films (Hong Kong), Aqua Prima (Turkey), Gulf Films Entertainment (Middle East), Investacommerce AG (the former Yugoslav territories), Destiny Films (France), Long Ride (Japan), MindJazz Pictures (Germany and Austria), New Cinema (Israel), Noori Pictures (South Korea), NOS Lusomundo Audiovisual (Portugal), NumeroUS Movie (China), Palace Entertainment (Australia and New Zealand), PVR Inox Pictures (India), Shaw Renters (Singapore), Swallow Wings Films (Taiwan), Vertice Cine S.L. (Spain), as well as through global airline distribution via Terry Steiner International.

	The docufilm is produced by Brunello Cucinelli S.p.A. and MasiFilm in collaboration with RAI Cinema.

schema.headline: Brunello Cucinelli Docufilm ‘The Gracious Visionary’ Sells Internationally Ahead of North American Release (EXCLUSIVE)

title: ‘Avengers: Doomsday’ Trailer: Doctor Doom Battles the MCU, X-Men and Avengers Face Off, Captain America Returns
description:
The first trailer for “Avengers: Doomsday” is finally here.

	After being shown to CinemaCon attendees in April, the full “Doomsday” trailer is now
body:
The first trailer for “Avengers: Doomsday” is finally here.

	After being shown to CinemaCon attendees in April, the full “Doomsday” trailer is now public and brings together nearly every hero in the MCU.

	“Doomsday” is the culmination of the MCU’s multiverse-crossing storytelling over the past seven years and three Phases since “Avengers: Endgame” released and became the second highest-grossing movie of all time. The team-up film brings together almost every major character from across the MCU to face its latest villain: Doctor Doom, played by Robert Downey Jr. in his Marvel return. 
	

	The trailer shows the Marvel heroes assembling as Doctor Doom prepares to wreak multiverse-crossing war on the heroes. Thor faces off against Doom and also reunites with Chris Evans’ Captain America, making his long-awaited return to the MCU after “Avengers: Endgame.” Several of Fox’s “X-Men” characters also officially cross over into the Avengers’ universe, including Professor Xavier, Magneto, Mystique, Cyclops and Channing Tatum’s Gambit from “Deadpool and Wolverine.” After hitting the big screen last year, the Fantastic Four and Thunderbolts are also back to help the Avengers save the multiverse.


	“Doomsday” hits theaters on Dec. 18, followed by “Avengers: Secret Wars” a year later. The Russo Brothers, after directing “Infinity War,” “Endgame” and two “Captain America” movies, are back to helm both upcoming “Avengers” films.

	After Iron Man sacrificed himself to defeat Thanos and save the world in “Endgame,” Downey Jr. is back — but not as his original, iconic Marvel role. The details for his turn as Doctor Doom haven’t been fully explained, but it’s likely that Doom is a variant of Iron Man from another universe. He briefly appeared, though his face was hidden, in the post-credits scene of “Fantastic Four: First Steps.”
	

	The list of which Marvel stars are returning for “Doomsday” is long, and they were all showcased in a slow-burn stunt in March 2025 that revealed the cast members’ names on the backs of chairs. Chris Hemsworth, Anthony Mackie, Danny Ramirez, Sebastian Stan, Paul Rudd, Tom Hiddleston, Letitia Wright, Winston Duke, Simu Liu and Tenoch Huerta are among the returning MCU characters for “Doomsday.” The Fantastic Four and Thunderbolts, the two most recent teams added alongside the Avengers, will bring back Pedro Pascal, Vanessa Kirby, Joseph Quinn, Ebon Moss-Bachrach, Florence Pugh, David Harbour, Wyatt Russell, Hannah John-Kamen and Lewis Pullman.

	Perhaps the most exciting castings for “Doomsday” are the past X-Men characters who are jumping into the MCU. They include Patrick Stewart, Ian McKellen, Kelsey Grammer, Alan Cumming, James Marsden, Channing Tatum and Rebecca Romijn.

	To build hype for “Doomsday,” Disney dropped four teaser previews tied to screenings of “Avatar: Fire and Ash” in late 2025 and early 2026. The first confirmed that Evans would return as Steve Rogers, aka Captain America, who now has a child; the second showed Hemsworth’s Thor and his adopted daughter Love; the third gave the first look at Stewart’s Prof. X, McKellen’s Magneto and Marsden’s Cyclops; and the fourth featured Wright’s Black Panther, Huerta’s Namor and Duke’s M’Baku meeting Moss Bachrach’s Thing in Wakanda.

	Watch the trailer below.

schema.headline: ‘Avengers: Doomsday’ Trailer: Doctor Doom Battles the MCU, X-Men and Avengers Face Off, Captain America Returns

title: Fox’s Red Seat Ventures, Amazon Strike Programmatic Advertising Partnership
description: Fox Corp.’s Red Seat Ventures is the latest entity to strike up a partnership with Amazon in a bid to give advertisers broader reach in programmatic
body:
Fox Corp.’s Red Seat Ventures is the latest entity to strike up a partnership with Amazon in a bid to give advertisers broader reach in programmatic venues. 

	Amazon will give advertisers the chance to buy up programmatic advertising inventory from Red Seat via its demand-side platform, the latest in a series of similar alliances media companies have been striking in recent months. Amazon has unveiled similar agreements with companies including Disney, Roku and Netflix, among others, over the past year.
	

	Under the new alliance, advertisers can gain access to commercial inventory tied to Red Seat’s portfolio of audio and video podcasts. Red Seat works with Megyn Kelly, Tucker Carlson and Bill O’Reilly, among others.


	“Our podcast audio and video creators have built some of the most trusted communities in media. Consumers keep returning to them, and that loyalty shapes discovery, consideration and brand affinity in ways algorithms alone can’t,” said Jeremy Price, senior vice president of sales at Red Seat Ventures, in a prepared statement. The alliance with Amazon offers “a new way to make creator-led media measurable, addressable and connected to real business outcomes.”

	Content companies striking DSP alliances hope the deals will give marketers the chance to buy a broader set of programmatic inventory from a single venue, potentially reducing the number of commercials they show to the same consumer and improve the effectiveness of commercials by distributing them with more precision

	Red Seat Ventures represents more than 40  creators who span genres including true crime, comedy, news, sports and entertainment.

schema.headline: Fox’s Red Seat Ventures, Amazon Strike Programmatic Advertising Partnership

title: ESPN Plunges Back Into Shohei Ohtani Money Transfer Scandal With New Tisha Thompson Podcast
description: Tisha Thompson in 2024 broke news about one of the biggest and most surprising scandals in sports — the transfer of large sums of money from basebal
body:
Tisha Thompson in 2024 broke news about one of the biggest and most surprising scandals in sports — the transfer of large sums of money from baseball great Shohei Otani’s bank account to illegal bookmakers. Two years later, she just might have more to say on the subject.

	Thompson will lead a six-part podcast series, “The Betrayal of Shohei Ohtani,” produced under ESPN’s long-running “30 for 30” banner. The podcast debuts July 28 with a drop of the first two episodes, with new chapters released  every Tuesday and Thursdays through August 11. The program not only examines the surprising incident but also the collapse of the friendship between Ohtani and Ippei Mizuhara, his interpreter and the person who used the pitcher’s money to cover gambling debts. Listeners will be able to hear, for the first time, audio from the only press interview Mizuhara ever granted.
	

	The conversation, says Thompson, represents “probably one of the most important interviews I’ve ever done in my career, and it is also the strangest interview I’ve ever done.”


	Sports fans were hooked on every last detail of the Ohtani imbroglio. Here, after all, was a player who had yet to demonstrate any landmark performance in the U.S., and who had in 2023 struck a 10-year contract with the Dodgers valued at $700 million. “This is all taking place in the lead-up to his debut in a Dodgers uniform, and Major League Baseball was going all in on showing that he was already the face” of the organization,” says Thompson. “He was the big, emerging star and so much money and marketing dollars had already been spent.”
	

	ESPN die-hards have long been accustomed to longer-form journalism from the Disney sports-media giant, not only from “30 for 30” but also programs such as “E:60.” Over the  years, ESPN has faced questions about how many more enterprise and investigative stories it can produce as it is forced to spend bigger sums on sports rights and as it draws closer to some of the sports leagues that supply the programs keeping fans watching. Disney has said that ESPN’s approach to journalism will not change even though the National Football League in 2025 took a 10% ownership stake in the sports outlet.

	To be sure, there are likely several parties that would prefer if Thompson just let the matter drop. At the same time, the Ohtani story continues to generate chatter and interest, she says, and there is more detail to offer. “We were able to get a ton of new reporting that people had never heard,” she says, and to broaden the tale by examining what the rise of wagering has done to the culture of sports.

	One of the people surfacing on the podcast is Matthew Bowyer, a bookmaker who ran an illegal gambling business — sports betting remains illegal in California — and counted Mizuhara among his clients.  Bowyer “is going to explain how he did it,” says Thompson, “how he hooked millionaire clients, where he went to look for them, how he lured them in and how he lured them to his sportsbook, how he would keep them on the line even though they were getting in over their heads.” Listeners will also hear from one of the investigators on the case, says Thompson, the “first time people who actually have first-hand knowledge about what happened will talk about what they saw and why they came to the conclusions that that they did.”

	She also hopes people who follow her story will come to understand how hard she and ESPN colleagues worked to make certain they were presenting factual material, and not idle chatter. “I’m not in the business of rumors. I’m in the business of presenting sourced information,” the journalist says. “This was a very challenging story. Investigative reporting is hard and part of the reason it’s hard is because it affects people’s lives and their reputations.”

	This program also has room for the offbeat. Listeners will learn of an association between Bowyer and one of the players on “The Real Housewives of Orange County.” Ryan Boyajian. Boyajian, then the fiancé of Jennifer Pedranit, was apparently wired Ohtani’s money. Boyajian received immunity in exchange for providing testimony, according to ESPN reporting.
	

	The story continues to generate interest, says Thompson, who notes she gets questions about it while visiting the dentist or while standing on the sidelines at kids’ sports events. “This is all they want to talk to me about.” This new examination of the Ohtani money scandal may fuel further conversation.

schema.headline: ESPN Plunges Back Into Shohei Ohtani Money Transfer Scandal With New Tisha Thompson Podcast

title: Jerusalem Film Festival Boss Urges Palestinian Filmmakers to Take Part: ‘The Cinemas Are Filled With Diverse Crowds — Jews and Arabs, Secular and Religious Citizens’
description: The boss of Israel’s Jerusalem Film Festival, Roni Mahadav-Levin, has written an open letter urging Palestinian filmmakers to take part in next year
body:
The boss of Israel’s Jerusalem Film Festival, Roni Mahadav-Levin, has written an open letter urging Palestinian filmmakers to take part in next year’s edition.

	“To artists around the world, and specifically to Palestinian filmmakers, my call to you is this: grab the opportunity to reach the public in our cinema halls,” Mahadav-Levin wrote in the open letter, which is published in full below.


	Of this year’s festival, which concluded Sunday, he said: “When the lights went down in our theaters, the audience was not a monolith. The cinemas were filled with diverse crowds, Jews and Arabs, secular and religious citizens, locals and internationals. For a few hours, they were united by a shared narrative on the screen. The debates that sparked in the lobby afterward, confronting uncomfortable truths about our society or sharing moments of profound empathy, were the building blocks of a resilient society. This is where hardline narratives are challenged. This is where the status quo is disrupted.”
	

	The 43rd edition of the Jerusalem Film Festival opened July 9 and ran until July 19. While there continue to be calls for boycott of all Israeli-linked film institutions, a number of Palestinian and Arab-Israeli filmmakers participated in this year’s Jerusalem Film Festival.

	Palestinian actor Ehab Elias Salami, who appears in Assaf Machnes’s feature “Where To?,” won the festival’s award for best acting performance. The festival also screened a compilation of short films, titled “All That Remains,” by six Palestinian filmmakers, curated by Fadi Far.
	

	Mahadav-Levin hopes more will participate next year.

	“Boycotts and cultural withdrawal do not punish the hardliners; they empower them,” he writes in his letter. “In a bitter irony, the hardliners who wish to censor art and the boycotters who seek to block audiences from seeing it end up sharing a common interest: both ultimately work to silence the discourse, isolate the public, and keep people divided. When you block your film from certain audiences, you are doing the censors’ work for them. Let your art confront, challenge, and inspire the audiences who need to engage with your perspectives right now.”

	Read the full letter from Roni Mahadav-Levin, executive director of the Jerusalem Cinematheque and the Jerusalem Film Festival, below:

	“On July 9, the 43rd edition of the Jerusalem Film Festival opened with the premiere of “Tell Me Everything” by director Moshe Rosenthal. Over the ensuing ten days, we were deeply honored to showcase the year’s most vital cinema, hosting a robust delegation of international directors, actors, and industry professionals.

	However, organizing a film festival in a complex, mixed city like Jerusalem, under Israel’s current hardline government, and following years in which a devastating conflict has deeply scarred our region, was not a task we took lightly.

	It should go without saying that the presence of global cinema and the more than thirty industry international guests, actors, directors, producers and scriptwriters, who chose to participate this year was not an endorsement of the Israeli government’s hardline policies. Rather, it was an affirmation of the power of culture, dialogue, and artistic exchange to resist the forces of extremism, challenge division, and keep democratic discourse alive.

	The threats to democracy we face today are not isolated to the Middle East. We watch the political chaos and deep polarization unfolding in the United States, we saw how creeping authoritarianism was overturned in Hungary, and we followed courageous, ongoing uprisings in Iran and elsewhere around the world. Across the globe, populist leaders and fundamentalist factions attempt to consolidate power by dividing populations and silencing dissent.

	Recent history, especially since the fall of the Berlin Wall, has taught us a vigorous lesson: dictatorships, authoritarian regimes, and populist governments are rarely dismantled by the force of external wars or diplomatic isolation. They are overthrown from within. Real change is driven by the people themselves, whether through the ballot box, however tempered the elections may be, or through widespread demonstrations and civil resistance.
	

	To empower those internal movements, we must keep the channels of cultural conversation wide open. The presence of dozens of international artists and filmmakers who joined us in Jerusalem this year is an acknowledgment that true exchange happens between individuals, regardless of their nationalities, alliances, or sociological identities. Throughout the festival, Israeli, Palestinian, and international stories were presented side by side, inviting audiences to engage with experiences, perspectives, and realities that might otherwise remain distant. Films, festival guests, and the festival itself do not represent nations or governments; they reflect the shared human condition.

	When the lights went down in our theaters, the audience was not a monolith. The cinemas were filled with diverse crowds, Jews and Arabs, secular and religious citizens, locals and internationals. For a few hours, they were united by a shared narrative on the screen. The debates that sparked in the lobby afterward, confronting uncomfortable truths about our society or sharing moments of profound empathy, were the building blocks of a resilient society. This is where hardline narratives are challenged. This is where the status quo is disrupted.

	If we truly believe in the universal values of humanity, civil rights, and freedom, we should aggressively encourage the exhibition of global cinema to citizens directly wherever they are. It is a powerful conduit for empathy and resistance. Therefore, when given the chance to bridge these divides, it is reckless to look the other way.

	To artists around the world, and specifically to Palestinian filmmakers, my call to you is this: grab the opportunity to reach the public in our cinema halls. With the Israeli public going to the polls in just three months, this remains a critical, fragile moment. It is both counterproductive and ultimately irresponsible to retreat into ideological purism by removing yourself from the cultural arena in the very place that needs your voice the most.

	Boycotts and cultural withdrawal do not punish the hardliners; they empower them. In a bitter irony, the hardliners who wish to censor art and the boycotters who seek to block audiences from seeing it end up sharing a common interest: both ultimately work to silence the discourse, isolate the public, and keep people divided. When you block your film from certain audiences, you are doing the censors’ work for them. Let your art confront, challenge, and inspire the audiences who need to engage with your perspectives right now.
	

	I truly hope that Israel, Palestine, and our entire region are heading toward better days soon. But getting there requires brave engagement, reflection, and art, not silence. Now is the time to lean in. Be part of this change. Be brave. Be heard.”

schema.headline: Jerusalem Film Festival Boss Urges Palestinian Filmmakers to Take Part: ‘The Cinemas Are Filled With Diverse Crowds — Jews and Arabs, Secular and Religious Citizens’

title: Liberty Mutual’s New Commercial Character Offers Insurance Against Ad-Avoiding Consumers
description:
What’s yellow, fluffy and has a name that’s likely to get stuck in your head? The insurance sector’s newest TV star.

	Liberty Mutual worked its way
body:
What’s yellow, fluffy and has a name that’s likely to get stuck in your head? The insurance sector’s newest TV star.

	Liberty Mutual worked its way into consumers’ brains over the past decade with a series of ads that feature a hard-to-forget slogan (Just repeat the first name of the company several times in sing-song fashion) along with a bizarre cast of characters that includes a “Limu Emu” and a guy named Doug. Starting this week, Liberty adds to the coterie, with “Liberty Biberty,” a fuzzy anthropomorphic character meant to symbolize the feeling a consumer gets when they nab insurance at a fair price.
	

	The character may look odd, but the strategy behind it is anything but.


	The expression “Liberty Biberty” has been featured in the insurance company’s campaigns since 2019, when one ad character known as “Bad Actor” uttered it as he tried to audition for a spot in the campaign. Now, says Jenna Lebel, chief marketing officer for U.S. retail market at Liberty, the saying a well-known piece of intellectual property the company can deploy smartly.



	“That phrase has just taken on a life of its own. It caught on quickly. People repeated it. They even call our company ‘Liberty Biberty’ sometimes,” she says. “I think brands can spend years and millions of dollars to try to build something like that — and we already have it. So rather than ignore it, we kind of leaned into it and that gave us an opportunity.”

	Add the creature to the swelling ranks of characters deployed in commercials to help insurance companies stand apart from one another. Over the decades, the average consumer has likely encountered Jake From State Farm; Progressive’s Flo and her colleagues Jamie and Mara, not to mention Dr. Rick; Allstate’s Mayhem; Geico’s Gecko; Farmers Insurance’s Professor Burke; and, of course, the famous Aflac Duck.
	

	Despite the seeming ubiquity of its advertising, Liberty is not the sector’s biggest backer of ads. The company is outspent widely by competitors such as State Farm, Geico, Allstate and Progressive, according to 2025 data from Dowling & Partners, a research firm that focuses on the insurance sector.

	Finding the right fictional character to talk up insurance has become key. “Unfortunately, as an insurance company, we don’t have a tangible product, and so I think that’s why you see the entire category rely on brand cues or brand devices like brand characters to really establish that linkage,” says Lebel. “Consumers, when they’re shopping for insurance, they’re shopping only a few brands, and it’s always the first few brands that come to mind. And so top-of-mind awareness or unawareness is hugely important in this category.”

	The characters are quirky, and, over many years, become part of a larger story as audiences learn more about them. At some point, however, even a favorite guest risks becoming unwelcome. That’s why many of the insurance companies keep adding to their roster of oddball personalities. Many of the companies may also fear the inevitable stage when consumers start to ignore ads because they’ve seen the same ones, or seen ones with the same themes, multiple times. That dynamic has played a bigger role in marketers’ decisions since the advent of the large streaming services, which often show fewer commercials overall, but can send the same ones to particular subscribers multiple times during a viewing experience.

	To stand out, Liberty Mutual tapped Jim Henson Creature Shop and puppeteer Grant Baciocco, who gives the new character its voice. “Puppets just bring so much joy and entertainment,” says Lebel, and the Henson people “are the experts in that area.” In weeks to come, sports fans may come across the new campaign not only in commercials but also integrations during NBC’s “Sunday Night Baseball”  and ESPN’s “Sports Center,”says Lebel. “We are still exploring” additional partnerships, she adds.

	The yellow creature was just one of the concepts Liberty Mutual considered. The company looked at dozens of different concepts, says Lebel, including using a unicorn, an alien visiting earth and a family of Statues of Liberty. Executives also consulted a panel that examined the reactions of thousands of consumers.
	

	Because consumers have already heard the “Biberty” phrase, says Lebel, the company believes there is less hard work to do to make the new ads connect. “We’re not manufacturing a mascot and hoping it sticks,” she says. “We’re taking something that people already know and repeat and just giving it a bigger role in the world.”

	Liberty isn’t elevating Biberty at the expense of other established spokes-characters. Viewers who follow the company’s commercials are likely to have new encounters with the LiMu Emu – and Doug.

schema.headline: Liberty Mutual’s New Commercial Character Offers Insurance Against Ad-Avoiding Consumers

title: ‘Satluj’ Review: A Moving Indian Activist Drama Pulled From Circulation
description: Honey Trehan’s “Satluj” — about a Sikh activist in the 1990s investigating extrajudicial killings — conjures heavy emotions despite its hagiographic
body:
Honey Trehan’s “Satluj” — about a Sikh activist in the 1990s investigating extrajudicial killings — conjures heavy emotions despite its hagiographic hues. Just as intriguing as its provocations are the controversies surrounding its release, starting with its unexplained removal from the Toronto International Film Festival lineup in 2023, then under the title “Punjab ’95.” This year, it finally evaded India’s censors on July 3rd, with a global digital release on the Indian streaming platform Zee5, though it was pulled for Indian viewers within 48 hours, and for international audiences the following week. However, this seemingly ancillary drama is central to the film’s mission statement, as an exposé of power and its suppression of truth.
	

	In a gloomy prologue, casual conversations between Punjab police officers precipitate equally casual executions by moonlight, under the pretext of curbing terror. Soon, the focus switches to an ordinary bank manager, Jaswant Singh Khalra (played by pop star Diljit Dosanjh), whose kindly demeanor sets him on a collision course with corrupt and dangerous forces. When an elderly aunt goes missing (after the police killing of her own husband), Khalra is driven to trace her whereabouts, though he ends up pulling on more threads than he bargained for.


	“Satluj” takes its name from a major river running through the state of Punjab, the banks of which become a keeper of terrible secrets, as a disposal ground for bodies. Although the plot is structured like a mystery, the goings-on beneath each shadowy façade may as well be out in the open: everyone knows local authorities have been disappearing dissenters, but they’re too afraid to object. However, the more questions Khalra asks, the more he unearths the scale, process and harrowing paper trail of this illicit machinery, including mass cremations of people still marked “missing” by police. It isn’t long before he switches to activism full time, in the hopes of bringing national and international attention to these killings, but this paints a sizable target on his back.
	

	Despite its didactic approach, Trehan’s drama trades in intimate textures, thanks in large part to Dosanjh’s measured performance as a man deeply stirred and disturbed by what he sees. The singer — whose recent North American tour landed him a guest spot on “The Tonight Show” — disappears into the role of an ego-less saint, whose presence isn’t so much biographical as it is liturgical. “Satluj” may engage in political discourse, but its primary lens is that of Sikh duty dichotomized with the casual cruelty of the state. Alongside cinematographer K. U. Mohanan and composer Marc Marder, Trehan formalizes this spiritual approach, through sounds and images with wrenching emotional impact that may as well emanate from the pit of your own stomach. The film frames spirituality as both a visceral response to injustice, and an unquenchable thirst to cure it.

	At 164 minutes in length, “Satluj” has it share of speed bumps. Vast swaths of time unfold in mere voiceover, and some investigations and proclamations feel repetitive, as the film reinforces its point ad nauseam. However, despite its drama verging on courtroom testimony (and in some cases, unfolding as actual courtroom statements, the lingua franca of the mainstream Indian social drama), Trehan maintains a delicate balance between the ethereal and the personal. He rounds out each scene with remarkable performers no matter the size of their role, or their character’s location on a strict moral axis, thus creating a discernible and familiar reality within the borders of his pedagogy. But even within the movie’s powerful melodrama sits a secondary tale of fascistic process, one that speaks truth to power through implication and whisper.

	Characters make frequent reference to political mood, and to major events in the 1980s — from assassinations to widespread riots — that thrust the Sikh community into the national spotlight. However, the film makes only passing mention of specifics, like the Khalistan separatist movement which forms the story’s backdrop, giving local police and politicians carte blanche for their brutality (and their persecution of Khalra). And yet, the machinery by which bystanders become dehumanized in “Satluj” speaks volumes about the default national perspective on these issues without giving them a name. When labels like “terrorist” and “insurgent” are bandied about, they become de facto death sentences. So while the film, on one hand, ignores the political perspective of its victims — including Khalra — its wide-spanning rebuttal to government overreach becomes more palatable to mass audiences in the process.
	

	The result has thus far spoken for itself. After years of censorious government demands  that include over 120 cuts, the version of “Satluj” out in the world is an uncompromised vision despite its bloat, and has seen rogue community screenings across India since its removal. The medium has become the message, ensuring that the very act of engaging with the film becomes akin to searching through records long-buried. With Dosanjh as his muse and moral conscience, Trehan constructs a sense of enormous occasion around each discovery, imbuing this tale of suppressed history and stripped personhood with the tremendous weight it deserves.

schema.headline: ‘Satluj’ Review: A Moving Indian Activist Drama Pulled From Circulation

title: Megan Rapinoe Is Launching Her Own Interview Podcast, ‘Why Are You Like This?’
description: Megan Rapinoe, soccer champion, activist and entrepreneur, is entering a new chapter with the forthcoming launch of weekly interview show “Why Are Y
body:
Megan Rapinoe, soccer champion, activist and entrepreneur, is entering a new chapter with the forthcoming launch of weekly interview show “Why Are You Like This?”

	The show, produced and distributed by Vox Media, will feature Rapinoe’s conversations with artists, athletes, activists and “architects of culture.” On the podcast, Rapinoe will try to get at what makes her guests tick — with discussions spanning “the personal and the professional, the origin story and the long game, the private cost and the public legacy,” per Vox Media.
	

	“Why Are You Like This?” is slated to premiere Thursday, Aug. 6, with new episodes dropping weekly. The show will be available on Spotify, Apple Podcasts, YouTube and other platforms. Each episode will run about one hour.


	Rapinoe, in an interview, declined to reveal who she has lined up for the pod but promises the show will provide a deep dive with “some of the world’s most interesting people.”

	“I am mostly interested in talking with people about why they do things the way they do things – and why some people seemingly take a left when everyone else is taking a right,” Rapinoe told Variety. “I’m interested in everybody’s lived experience. … I’m curious to see how they see themselves in the world.”

	Rapinoe is a two-time World Cup champion and Olympic gold medalist. She helped lead the U.S. Women’s National Soccer Team to the 2019 FIFA Women’s World Cup Championship, scoring some of the biggest goals of the tournament, and took home the tournament’s two top honors: the Golden Boot for top scorer and the Golden Ball for best overall player in the tournament. She is also a New York Times bestselling author of memoir “One Life” and is a Presidential Medal of Freedom award recipient.
	

	Rapinoe previously co-hosted women’s sports podcast “A Touch More” with pro basketball player Sue Bird, also produced in partnership with Vox Media. Rapinoe continues to produce through the A Touch More banner with Bird. (Rapinoe and Bird announced their separation as a couple in April.)

	On “Why Are You Like This?”, Rapinoe expects she and her guests will occasionally delve into women’s sports, including the 2027 Women’s World Cup. But she says the show’s scope is much broader than sports alone. “It’s about people who have influence on culture in different ways: what’s hard for them, what’s scary for them, what their origin stories are,” she said.

	Vox Media will oversee production, sales, marketing and distribution for “Why Are You Like This?” Rapinoe’s new show joins the lineup of other Vox Media Podcast Network shows, which include “Pivot” from Kara Swisher and Scott Galloway; “Pretty Tough” from tennis pro Maria Sharapova; “Stay Tuned” with former Manhattan U.S. attorney Preet Bharara; and “The Curiosity Shop” from Brené Brown and Adam Grant.

	“We’re thrilled to be continuing our partnership with Megan, an iconoclast who’s as admired for her fearless voice as for her accomplishments in sports,” said Nishat Kurwa, SVP and executive producer at Vox Media. “’Why Are You Like This?’ is an exciting opportunity to leverage her range and authority to reveal the layered backstories of her fellow trailblazers.”

	The Vox Media Podcast Network is now owned by James Murdoch’s Lupa Systems, which acquired it in May along with New York Magazine (including Vulture and The Cut) and Vox.com for more than $300 million. Separately, PMC (parent of Variety) announced a deal to acquire other Vox Media digital brands, including Eater, The Verge, SB Nation, Popsugar, The Dodo, Punch and Thrillist.

schema.headline: Megan Rapinoe Is Launching Her Own Interview Podcast, ‘Why Are You Like This?’

title: Celebrities Eating Ice Cream For National Ice Cream Day
description:
National Ice Cream Day
                          Celebs Scream for Ice Cream!
body:
National Ice Cream Day
                          Celebs Scream for Ice Cream!
                      
      
    
      
        
              
    
    
            It's National Ice Cream Day ... and you're not the only one screaming for ice cream ... Hollywood is joining the chorus too!!!
      
    
            That's because even A-listers aren't immune to a little sweet treat, especially as temps pick up this summer.
      
    
            Celebs like Sydney Sweeney and Ryan Seacrest have been snapped indulging in a little ice cream. Tyra Banks couldn't be beat, though ... triple-fisting three cones at once!
      
    
            But they aren't the only stars who love satisfying their sweet tooth ... check out our gallery to see all the ice cream lovers!

title: PlaqueBoyMax Ends Streamer University Broadcast After Heated 'Diva' Clash
description:
Streamer University
                          PlaqueBoyMax Ends Stream After Heated 'Diva' Rant
body:
Streamer University
                          PlaqueBoyMax Ends Stream After Heated 'Diva' Rant
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              July 19, 2026
                            5:58 PM PDT
                                                                            
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            PlaqueBoyMax pulled the plug on his 24/7 Streamer University broadcast ... after fellow streamer OjaySuave ripped into his "Diva" persona and accused him of selling out for clicks.
      
    
        Waiting for your permission to load the Twitter Tweet.
      
    
            The heated exchange went down Saturday night during Ojay’s mock lecture on "How to be a Diva," when he told Max he needed guidance and a "church bath" after recently getting a "Diva" tattoo.
      
    
        Waiting for your permission to load the Twitter Tweet.
      
    
            Ojay repeatedly told Max he was a grown man and "not no Diva," accusing him of clip farming and warning him not to lose himself or sell his soul. Watch the video ... because the supposed advice quickly turned personal.
      

                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Max fired back ... defending his right to express himself and telling viewers to embrace what makes them different. He also questioned why someone who truly cared about him would air those concerns in front of hundreds of people instead of pulling him aside privately.
      
    
            An emotional Max then ended his around-the-clock stream and left to cool off.
      
    
        Waiting for your permission to load the Twitter Tweet.
      
    
            The drama didn’t end there ... streamer Wardrobe later confronted Ojay, accusing him of bullying Max and demanding a public apology. Ojay insisted he was joking and didn’t realize Max would take his comments so seriously.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Of course, Streamer University is Kai Cenat's creator boot camp ... bringing internet personalities together on a mock college campus to collaborate, compete and learn the content game.
      
    
            Be sure to watch the clips ... Streamer U just delivered a lesson in keeping some conversations off-camera.
      
      
              
          
                          
                Kai Cenat

title: Lady Gaga Celebrates Manager Bobby Campbell's Wedding With Fiancé
description:
Lady Gaga & Michael Polansky
                          Date Night for Her Manager's Big 'I Do'
body:
Lady Gaga & Michael Polansky
                          Date Night for Her Manager's Big 'I Do'
                      
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Lady Gaga was in celebration mode this weekend ... 'cause TMZ has learned she and her fiancé, Michael Polansky, stepped out for date night at the wedding of her longtime manager and close friend, Bobby Campbell, and his husband.
      
    
            Sources with direct knowledge tell TMZ ... Gaga and Polansky attended the Saturday night ceremony at the Walt Disney Concert Hall in downtown Los Angeles, where Campbell and Robert García tied the knot surrounded by family and friends.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            We're told Gaga kept a low profile throughout the evening, blending in with the rest of the guests instead of stealing the spotlight. And despite her superstar status, our sources say she did not perform during the reception.
      

            Instead, we're told "Gypsy Woman" singer Crystal Waters provided the night's entertainment, getting guests on their feet during the celebration.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Campbell and Gaga go way back. He's managed the pop icon for years and has helped steer some of the biggest moments of her career, from chart-topping albums and global tours to her Oscar-winning era and recent projects.
      
    
            Their relationship has long extended beyond business, with Gaga frequently praising Campbell publicly and the two sharing a close friendship.
      
      
              
          
                          
                Michael Polansky
              
                          
                Lady Gaga

schema.headline: Lady Gaga Celebrates Manager Bobby Campbell's Wedding With Fiancé

title: J.D. Vance and Wife Usha Welcome Baby Boy, Make Vice Presidential History
description:
J.D. And Usha Vance
                          Welcome to the Family, Baby Alec!!!
body:
J.D. And Usha Vance
                          Welcome to the Family, Baby Alec!!!
                      
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Vice President J.D. Vance and Second Lady Usha Vance just added another little Vance to the family ... welcoming their fourth child and making a little history in the process.
      
    
            The couple announced Sunday their baby boy, Alec Neel Vance, was born earlier in the morning ... saying both mom and Alec are happy and healthy, while their three older children are "overjoyed" to meet their new brother.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            The birth is also one for the history books -- Alec is reportedly the first child born to a sitting U.S. vice president in more than 150 years. The last was Schuyler Colfax III, whose father was serving as VP when he arrived in 1870.
      

            Alec joins siblings Ewan, 9, Vivek, 6, and Mirabel, 4 ... giving the vice president a full house at the Naval Observatory.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            J.D. and Usha thanked the doctors and staff at Walter Reed National Military Medical Center, along with the White House medical team, for caring for their growing family.
      
    
            The new arrival fits right in with J.D.'s outspoken push for Americans to have more kids ... he declared at the 2025 March for Life rally, "I want more babies in the United States of America."
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Usha announced her pregnancy in January and later got plenty of attention for rocking an $8.75 Old Navy maternity dress -- prompting J.D. to jokingly nominate his bargain-hunting wife as the country's next budget director.
      
    
            The VP has wanted more babies in America -- and the Vances are leading by example.
      
      
              
          
                          
                J.D. Vance
              
                          
                Family
              
                          
                White House
              
                          
                Politics
              
                          
                News

schema.headline: J.D. Vance and Wife Usha Welcome Baby Boy, Make Vice Presidential History
